daby_huang.name: Daby Huang
daby_huang.summary:
Out of Oregon, Daby Huang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Daby's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Daby has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Daby is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Daby has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 32 different players across the career:
  • Krish Ashok (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Hudson Abidin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)
  • Meghana Nishant (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

Having moved up to U19, Daby is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Daby brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Daby has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Daby has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Daby has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
daby_huang.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Daby Huang, from Oregon, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-5. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Daby has 65 career entries across 30 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Daby saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daby's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 13 entries — a stretch where Daby actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Daby is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Daby would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Daby has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Daby has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Daby's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daby plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Daby is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Daby and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Daby needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 32 of them, to be precise. Daby has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daby's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Allison Hsu: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Allison Hsu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Vincent Chen has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Daby's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Daby has shown up 8 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Daby has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daby is not just losing locally — Daby is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Daby can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daby has 37 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daby showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Daby traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Daby entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Daby paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Daby's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 64 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Daby is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Daby figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Daby's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

daisy_ma.name: Daisy Ma
daisy_ma.summary:
Out of California, Daisy Ma has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 20 entries in 7 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Daisy competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Daisy has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Jake Shi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Daisy has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Daisy isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Daisy's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
daisy_ma.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Daisy Ma, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 20 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 20-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Daisy's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Daisy keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Daisy saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daisy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Daisy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daisy's texts. The go-to partner is Jake Shi (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Marcus Chen has emotionally recovered.

Daisy has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daisy has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daisy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Daisy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Daisy entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Daisy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Daisy keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Daisy has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Daisy, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

daisy_tang.name: Daisy Tang
daisy_tang.summary:
Representing New York, Daisy Tang is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Daisy especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Daisy has competed at both U11 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

daisy_xu.name: Daisy Xu
daisy_xu.summary:
A California competitor, Daisy Xu is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GD U13, GS U13) puts Daisy in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Daisy is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Daisy especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 5 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U13, GS U13) • 1 ORC title.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Daisy adapts to anyone:
  • Hanyi (Sissi) Lei (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (6 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Joey Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #2)

Daisy has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Daisy has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Daisy has been seeded #1 or #2 in 8 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Daisy is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
daisy_xu.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Daisy Xu file just landed on our desk. Daisy hails from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Daisy has 69 career entries across 25 tournaments and has managed to win 7 of them — a 10% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Daisy saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daisy's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Daisy actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

GS U13 used to be Daisy's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 Arena SoCal ORC era. Now Daisy is grinding for a top-2 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

In Mixed Doubles, Daisy has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Daisy chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically.

The seeding trajectory in GS U13 tells a story: #1 at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC), down to #2 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. When the people whose job is to rank you start ranking you lower, that is not a slump — that is a trend line with a very clear direction, and it is not pointing up.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Daisy experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Daisy has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Daisy entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Daisy does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Daisy has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 7 wins means Daisy is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daisy plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 0 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Daisy can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Daisy has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daisy's texts. The go-to partner is Yian Yang (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Vishnu Anand has emotionally recovered.

Of Daisy's 7 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Daisy feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 9 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Daisy has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Then there is the seeding situation. Daisy has been seeded in 18 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Daisy takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Daisy. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Daisy of is playing it safe. Daisy has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Daisy has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Daisy has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daisy is not just losing locally — Daisy is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xu family travels cross-country so Daisy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daisy has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daisy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Daisy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Daisy entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Daisy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Daisy story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 7 titles across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. But every season Daisy comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Daisy, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Daiwik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Daiwik entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Daiwik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Daiwik story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Daiwik comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Daiwik, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

daksh_mannadath.name: Daksh Mannadath
daksh_mannadath.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Daksh Mannadath has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 9 entries across 5 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Daksh competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

In doubles, Daksh has formed effective partnerships:
  • Soham Kulkarni (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Daksh is still ascending.

Daksh has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
daksh_mannadath.roast:
Allow us to introduce Daksh Mannadath, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 win across 9 entries in 5 tournaments. That is a 11% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Daksh shows up to a tournament, Daksh pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Daksh saga began at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC with 1 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2026 Seattle NW CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daksh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Daksh's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daksh has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daksh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U15, which means Daksh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, where Daksh entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Daksh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Daksh story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Daksh comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Daksh, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

daksh_thakur.name: Daksh Thakur
daksh_thakur.summary:
Representing Texas, Daksh Thakur has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U11 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Daksh's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Daksh competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-3 finisher.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Daksh boasts 36 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a clutch 56% win rate in three-game matches (10/18), a biggest win margin of 37 points.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Daksh adapts to anyone:
  • Srinivas Shankar (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Arjun Sharma — 2 events in BD, best: #4

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 3 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Daksh is still ascending.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Daksh isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Daksh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
daksh_thakur.roast:
Step right up and meet Daksh Thakur, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 3 wins across 25 entries in 12 tournaments. That is a 12% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Daksh shows up to a tournament, Daksh pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Daksh saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daksh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 12 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daksh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Daksh can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Daksh has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Srinivas Shankar (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Daksh's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Daksh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daksh has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daksh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in BS U11, which means Daksh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 DFW South OLC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles, 25 entries, and 22 that ended without a trophy. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Daksh looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Daksh. We will be watching.

daksha_ravindhar.name: Daksha Ravindhar
daksha_ravindhar.summary:
A California competitor, Daksha Ravindhar is a committed competitor who keeps showing up, currently competing at the U17 level, with 21 career entries across 8 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Daksha's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

Notably, Daksha has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Daksha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Daksha has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Daksha has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Daksha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
daksha_ravindhar.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Daksha Ravindhar file just landed on our desk. Daksha hails from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Daksha has 21 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Daksha saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daksha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Daksha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daksha's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Dwijesh Makireddi has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Daksha has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Daksha has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daksha is not just losing locally — Daksha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ravindhar family travels cross-country so Daksha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daksha has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daksha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Daksha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Daksha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Daksha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 21 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Daksha is not most humans. Daksha is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Daksha the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Daksha. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U11, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Daniel keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Daniel has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Daniel, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

daniel_li.name: Daniel Li
daniel_li.summary:
Representing California, Daniel Li has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 44 entries in 17 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Daniel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Daniel adapts to anyone:
  • Morgan Lu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (8 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Emma Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Allen Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Daniel has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Daniel has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Daniel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
daniel_li.roast:
Step right up and meet Daniel Li, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 44 career entries in 17 tournaments, Daniel has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 43 entries where Daniel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 44 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Daniel saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 44 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Daniel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daniel's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Morgan Lu: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Morgan Lu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Chuqi Julie Jin has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Daniel entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Daniel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Daniel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Daniel has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daniel is not just losing locally — Daniel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Daniel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 44 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Daniel. Daniel just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Daniel has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Daniel. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Daniel: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

daniel_seungchan_park.name: Daniel Seungchan Park
daniel_seungchan_park.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Daniel Seungchan Park is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Daniel in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Daniel is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Daniel especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U15 BS) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U17) • 3 ORC titles.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Daniel adapts to anyone:
  • Adhyaan Balaji (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in BD/U17, across 2 seasons)
  • Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (5 events, across 2 seasons)

Daniel has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Daniel has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Daniel has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 9 titles, Daniel has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
daniel_seungchan_park.roast:
Allow us to introduce Daniel Seungchan Park, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. And yes, Daniel IS a Junior National Champion — BD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 69-entry career across 27 tournaments with a 13% win rate, which means 60 times — 60! — Daniel went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Daniel saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Daniel burst onto the scene with a title in XD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Daniel has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Daniel promised and what Daniel has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 5 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Daniel actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Daniel is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

BS U17 used to be Daniel's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai era. Now Daniel is grinding for a top-17 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away. In BS U19, Daniel was once THE player to beat — 1 title including at 2024 SBC NW CRC. These days, Daniel is finishing 2nd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Daniel reaches a final. Daniel has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Daniel does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Daniel has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 9 wins means Daniel is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 5 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Daniel can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Daniel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daniel's texts. The go-to partner is Adhyaan Balaji (6 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Rushil Wadhwani has emotionally recovered.

Of Daniel's 9 career titles, 5 came at local events versus 4 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Daniel feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Daniel entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Daniel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Daniel and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Daniel has been seeded in 20 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Daniel takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Daniel. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Daniel of is playing it safe. Daniel has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Daniel has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Daniel has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daniel is not just losing locally — Daniel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Park family travels cross-country so Daniel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 2nd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Daniel story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 9 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who proof that glory has an expiration date and Daniel's is past due. But every season Daniel comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Daniel, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

daniel_su.name: Daniel Su
daniel_su.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Daniel Su has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 15 entries across 7 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Daniel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Daniel has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Jonathan Wu (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

A veteran of the junior circuit, Daniel has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Daniel has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
daniel_su.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Daniel Su, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 win across 15 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 7% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Daniel shows up to a tournament, Daniel pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Daniel saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE OLC (NVBC) in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daniel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 5 entries — a stretch where Daniel actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Daniel is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Daniel is 0-for-7. Zero titles in 7 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Daniel and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Daniel needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Daniel has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BD U13 — we hope Aobin Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Daniel's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in BS U13, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13), where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Daniel story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Daniel comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Daniel, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

daniel_t_lee.name: Daniel T Lee
daniel_t_lee.summary:
Hailing from California, Daniel T Lee is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U13 level, with 12 entries across 6 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Daniel is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Daniel has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Daniel's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
daniel_t_lee.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daniel T Lee, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Daniel's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Daniel keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Daniel saga began at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Daniel has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Daniel keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Daniel has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Daniel knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

daniel_wu.name: Daniel Wu
daniel_wu.summary:
Hailing from California, Daniel Wu keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 13 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

Notably, Daniel has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Daniel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Daniel adapts to anyone:
  • David Purnomo (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Yuanchen Wu — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Daniel has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Daniel has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Daniel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
daniel_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daniel Wu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 31 career entries in 13 tournaments, Daniel has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 30 entries where Daniel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 31 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Daniel saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 31 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Daniel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daniel's texts. The longest-running partnership is with David Purnomo: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope David Purnomo has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Yuanchen Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Daniel entered 7 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Daniel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Daniel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Daniel has shown up 5 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Daniel has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daniel is not just losing locally — Daniel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Daniel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 31 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Daniel is not most humans. Daniel is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Daniel the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Daniel. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

daniel_xi.name: Daniel Xi
daniel_xi.summary:
Out of California, Daniel Xi is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U15 level, with 11 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Daniel especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Daniel adapts to anyone:
  • Lokesh Ravuri (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

daniel_xi.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Daniel Xi, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Daniel has 11 career entries across 4 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Daniel saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Daniel has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Chloe Ye has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Daniel has shown up 6 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Daniel has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Daniel entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 97th. That is an average placement of 62nd, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Daniel keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Daniel has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Daniel knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.
