daniel_xie.name: Daniel Xie
daniel_xie.summary:
Hailing from California, Daniel Xie knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 47 entries across 17 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Daniel especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Daniel has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Bernice Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (10 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Krish Raja (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U15 (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Christopher Gunawan (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

The 2023-2024 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Daniel is still ascending.

Daniel has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Daniel has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Daniel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
daniel_xie.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daniel Xie, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 47 career entries in 17 tournaments, Daniel has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 46 entries where Daniel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 47 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Daniel saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) in BD U15 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daniel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Daniel is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Daniel and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Daniel needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Daniel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Daniel's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Bernice Xu: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Bernice Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sraavya Aduri has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Daniel's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Daniel entered 6 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Daniel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Daniel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Daniel has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daniel is not just losing locally — Daniel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xie family travels cross-country so Daniel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Daniel entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Daniel's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 46 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Daniel is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Daniel figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Daniel's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Daniel brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Daniel Zhang, from parts unknown, continues to exist, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Daniel is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 SBC NW CRC, and so far, the only thing Daniel has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

Let's dig in.

The Daniel saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Daniel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 8 entries — a stretch where Daniel actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Daniel is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If Daniel's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Daniel has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Daniel does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Daniel has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Daniel is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Daniel can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Daniel has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.'

Daniel has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U19, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

In the end, Daniel's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 12 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Daniel is a player who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. Will Daniel figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Daniel's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

daniel_zhu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daniel Zhu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Daniel is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Daniel has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Daniel saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. Daniel is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Daniel wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

danny_luo.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Danny Luo, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Danny has 29 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Danny saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Danny plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Danny has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Danny's texts. The longest-running partnership is with William Lin: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope William Lin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Owen Wang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Danny has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Danny has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Danny has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Danny has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Danny showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Danny traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Danny entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Danny paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Danny keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Danny has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Danny knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Danny changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

daphne_huang.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Daphne Huang, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 21 career entries in 8 tournaments, Daphne has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Daphne drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 21 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Daphne saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 7th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daphne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Daphne has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Stella Pan: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Stella Pan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U17 — we hope Stella Pan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Daphne entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Daphne's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Daphne and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Daphne has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daphne is not just losing locally — Daphne is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Daphne can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daphne has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daphne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U17, which means Daphne traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Daphne entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Daphne paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Daphne keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Daphne has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Daphne, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Darmikh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Darmikh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 24 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Darmikh is not most humans. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Darmikh the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Darmikh. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Darren has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Darren. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Darren: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

darren_hoesan.name: Darren Hoesan
darren_hoesan.summary:
A California competitor, Darren Hoesan is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 3 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Darren competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
darren_hoesan.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Darren Hoesan, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Darren is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Darren has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Darren saga began at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Darren has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darren has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darren showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15, which means Darren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Darren entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Darren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

With only 3 entries on the books, Darren is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Darren. We will have more material. We always do.

darren_teu.name: Darren Teu
darren_teu.summary:
Representing Texas, Darren Teu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Darren's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Darren competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (10 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Darren adapts to anyone:
  • Yelang Lee (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (6 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ansen Liu (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in BD/U19, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Darren has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Darren has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Darren is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
darren_teu.roast:
Step right up and meet Darren Teu, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 52 career entries in 19 tournaments, Darren has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 51 entries where Darren drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 52 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Darren saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in BD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Darren's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 11 entries — a stretch where Darren actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Darren is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Darren plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Darren is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Darren and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Darren needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Darren has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Darren's texts. The go-to partner is Ansen Liu (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Geoffray Lee has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Darren's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Darren entered 10 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Darren's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Darren and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Darren has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Darren is not just losing locally — Darren is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Teu family travels cross-country so Darren can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darren has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darren showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Darren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Darren entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Darren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title, 52 entries, and 51 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Darren looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Darren. We will be watching.

Darren has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Darren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Darren entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Darren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Darren story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jain family travels cross-country so Darsh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darsh has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darsh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13, which means Darsh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Darsh entered 3 events with results ranging from 2nd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Darsh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles, 39 entries, and 36 that ended without a trophy. A player who has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the moments of brilliance — Darsh looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Darsh. We will be watching.

darsh_sree_gadepalli.name: Darsh Sree Gadepalli
darsh_sree_gadepalli.summary:
Out of Texas, Darsh Sree Gadepalli is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U13 level, with 6 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Darsh competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Darsh has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
darsh_sree_gadepalli.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Darsh Sree Gadepalli, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Darsh is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 South Frisco ORC, and so far, the only thing Darsh has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Darsh saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darsh has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darsh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BD U13, which means Darsh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Darsh entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Darsh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Darsh keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Darsh has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Darsh, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

darshan_karthi.name: Darshan Karthi
darshan_karthi.summary: Darshan Karthi is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Darshan's.
darshan_karthi.roast:
Step right up and meet Darshan Karthi, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Darshan is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 NorCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Darshan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Darshan saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Darshan is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Darshan's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Darshan is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darshan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darshan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. Darshan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Darshan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

david_purnomo.name: David Purnomo
david_purnomo.summary:
Hailing from Washington, David Purnomo has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and David's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, David has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

David is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

David has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Daniel Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Kavya Krishna (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

David has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

David has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, David brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
david_purnomo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for David Purnomo, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 35 career entries in 13 tournaments, David has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 34 entries where David drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 35 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The David saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where David burst onto the scene with a title in BD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, David has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what David promised and what David has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where David actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and David is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In Boys' Singles, David has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently David chokes in Boys' Singles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the David experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. David has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NW CRC, where David entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, David does own 1 actual title, so it is not like David has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means David is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. David plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — David is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside David and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: David needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. David has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers David's texts. The go-to partner is Thomas Wang (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Krisha Tyagi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of David's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — David entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at David's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at David and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, David has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

David has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because David is not just losing locally — David is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Purnomo family travels cross-country so David can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. David has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where David showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means David traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where David entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying David paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The David story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. But every season David comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, David, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

david_zhang.roast:
Allow us to introduce David Zhang, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. David has 50 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The David saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, David would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times David has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times David has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in David's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. David plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 50 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. David has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers David's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Derek Huang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Derek Huang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Venba Senkadir has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, David has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because David is not just losing locally — David is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so David can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. David has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where David showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means David traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where David entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying David paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 50 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But David is not most humans. David is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes David the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, David. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

david_zuo.name: David Zuo

David has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

David has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 18 seeded entries across the career, David consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, David is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

deeksha_kasala.name: Deeksha Kasala
deeksha_kasala.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Deeksha Kasala has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 5 entries across 2 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Deeksha competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #3, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Deeksha has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Mokshagna Surpur (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD
deeksha_kasala.roast:
Allow us to introduce Deeksha Kasala, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 5 entries in 2 tournaments. That is a 20% conversion rate, which means roughly 8 out of every 10 times Deeksha shows up to a tournament, Deeksha pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Deeksha saga began at 2025 Fortius South CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC (Dec) in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Deeksha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Deeksha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Deeksha is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Deeksha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Deeksha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Deeksha is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Deeksha wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

deekshith_vasanth.roast:
Step right up and meet Deekshith Vasanth, from Oregon, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Deekshith's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Deekshith keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Deekshith saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Deekshith has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.'

Deekshith has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Deekshith has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Deekshith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U13, which means Deekshith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 NW OLC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, where Deekshith entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Deekshith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 12 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Deekshith is not most humans. Deekshith is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Deekshith the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Deekshith. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

  • Roger Kung (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Elena Zhong (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Xirui Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)

Deepika has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Deepika brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
deepika_vaishnavi_venkatraman.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Deepika Vaishnavi Venkatraman, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Deepika has 37 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Deepika saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Deepika would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Deepika has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Deepika has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Deepika's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Deepika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Deepika has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Deepika's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Andy Starostin: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Andy Starostin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Andy Starostin has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Deepika has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Deepika has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Deepika is not just losing locally — Deepika is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Venkatraman family travels cross-country so Deepika can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Deepika has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Deepika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Deepika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Deepika entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Deepika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Deepika keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Deepika has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Deepika knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Deepika changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Dennis adapts to anyone:
  • Nicholas Volfbeyn — a trusted BD partner (12 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Dennis has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Dennis has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Dennis brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
dennis_shao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dennis Shao, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Dennis has 43 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Dennis saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dennis plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 43 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Dennis has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dennis's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Nicholas Volfbeyn: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Dennis has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dennis is not just losing locally — Dennis is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shao family travels cross-country so Dennis can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dennis has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dennis showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Dennis traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Dennis entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Dennis paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 43 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Dennis is not most humans. Dennis is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Dennis the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Dennis. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.
