derek_huang.name: Derek Huang
derek_huang.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Derek Huang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Derek's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Derek especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Derek boasts a solid 65% win rate over 71 matches, a positive +366 point differential across the career, a clutch 57% win rate in three-game matches (8/14).

Derek has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Seanna Yang (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (15 total events in XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • David Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Leslie Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

Derek has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Derek has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Derek consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 4 titles, Derek has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
derek_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Derek Huang, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Derek has 59 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Derek saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 OBA NW OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Derek's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Boys' Doubles finals and Derek — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Derek has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Derek's trophies to save time. Can we talk about Derek and Boys' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 1 losses. Derek can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Derek still cannot reach it.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Derek reaches a final. Derek has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SBC NW CRC, where Derek entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Derek does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Derek has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Derek is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Derek can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Derek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Derek's texts. The go-to partner is Seanna Yang (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Iniyan Arun has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Derek's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Derek has shown up 9 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Derek a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Derek has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 OBA NW OLC in BS U11, where Derek was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Derek is not just losing locally — Derek is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Derek can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Derek entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Derek's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 4 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 55 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Derek is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. Will Derek figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Derek's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

derek_li.name: Derek Li
derek_li.summary:
Representing California, Derek Li has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 22 entries in 11 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Derek is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Derek adapts to anyone:
  • Calum Lim (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)
  • Mahir s Modi — 3 events in BD, best: #3, across 2 seasons

Derek has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Derek has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Derek brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
derek_li.roast:
Step right up and meet Derek Li, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Derek has 22 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Derek saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 22 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Derek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Derek's texts. The go-to partner is Mahir s Modi (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Calum Lim has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Derek has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Derek is not just losing locally — Derek is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Derek can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Derek entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Derek keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Derek has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Derek knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Derek changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. derek_ruoyu_li.name: Derek Ruoyu Li
derek_ruoyu_li.summary:
Out of New Jersey, Derek Ruoyu Li has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 28 entries across 13 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Derek is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Derek adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Derek has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Derek has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
derek_ruoyu_li.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Derek Ruoyu Li, from New Jersey, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 28 entries in 13 tournaments. That is a 4% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Derek shows up to a tournament, Derek pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Derek saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Derek's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 11 entries — a stretch where Derek actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Derek is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Boys' Doubles finals and Derek — name a more iconic tragedy. 4 trips to the championship match, and Derek has turned 4 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Derek's trophies to save time.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Derek reaches a final. Derek has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Derek does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Derek has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Derek is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Derek is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Derek and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Derek needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Derek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Derek's texts. The go-to partner is Ethan Chen (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Will Luo has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Derek's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Derek has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Derek entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title, 28 entries, and 27 that ended without a trophy. A player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Derek looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Derek. We will be watching.

So from the bottom of our hearts, Derik: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

derrick_cheung.name: Derrick Cheung
derrick_cheung.summary:
A New York competitor, Derrick Cheung is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U15 level, with 17 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Derrick is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Derrick has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Zhuo Xu Zhou — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Derrick has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Derrick brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
derrick_cheung.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Derrick Cheung file just landed on our desk. Derrick hails from New York, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Derrick's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Derrick keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Derrick saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derrick plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Derrick has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Zhuo Xu Zhou (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Zhuo Xu Zhou has emotionally recovered.

Derrick has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derrick has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derrick showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Derrick traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Derrick entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Derrick paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 17 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Derrick. Derrick just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Derrick has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Derrick. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Derrick: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

devaansh_mahato.name: Devaansh Mahato
devaansh_mahato.summary:
Hailing from California, Devaansh Mahato has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Devaansh's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Devaansh is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Devaansh boasts a career +556 point differential, a clutch 59% win rate in three-game matches (10/17), a biggest win margin of 40 points.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Devaansh adapts to anyone:
  • Venba Senkadir (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Timothy Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Isha Suresh (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3)
  • Timmy Qinnuo Fan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #4)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Devaansh is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Devaansh has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Devaansh has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Devaansh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Devaansh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
devaansh_mahato.roast:
Allow us to introduce Devaansh Mahato, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Devaansh has 39 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Devaansh saga began at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Devaansh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Devaansh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Devaansh is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In Boys' Singles, Devaansh has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Devaansh chokes in Boys' Singles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Devaansh reaches a final. Devaansh has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC, where Devaansh entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Devaansh does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Devaansh has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Devaansh is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Devaansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Devaansh is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Devaansh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Devaansh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Devaansh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Devaansh's texts. The go-to partner is Venba Senkadir (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Hector Jingbo Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Devaansh's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Devaansh has shown up 5 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Devaansh is not just losing locally — Devaansh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mahato family travels cross-country so Devaansh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devaansh has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devaansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Devaansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Devaansh entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 34th, which is a polite way of saying Devaansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Devaansh's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 37 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Devaansh is a player who has turned Boys' Singles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Devaansh figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Devaansh's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Devansh can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Devansh's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Devansh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devansh has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U11, which means Devansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Devansh entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Devansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

With only 10 entries on the books, Devansh is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Devansh. We will have more material. We always do.

devin_garg.name: Devin Garg
devin_garg.summary:
Representing California, Devin Garg stands as an ORC champion, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Devin is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: 2 ORC titles.

Devin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Muyang Li — 3 events in BD, best: #3

A veteran of the junior circuit, Devin has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Devin has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Devin consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Devin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
devin_garg.roast:
Step right up and meet Devin Garg, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. An Open Regional Championship titlist with 2 ORC titles, which sounds very official — and it is, ORCs are the real deal. But let's zoom out: that's 2 wins out of 27 ORC entries, and an overall career of 2-for-29 (7%). Regional royalty, national afterthought. A big deal on the local circuit, a footnote everywhere else. Let's dig in.

The Devin saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Devin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Devin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Devin is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Devin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Devin is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Devin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Devin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Devin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Devin's texts. The go-to partner is Ella Mak (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Rubie Liu has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Devin is not just losing locally — Devin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Garg family travels cross-country so Devin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devin has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U17, which means Devin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NW ORC, where Devin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Devin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Devin story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Devin comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Devin, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Devin has 46 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Devin saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Devin burst onto the scene with a title in BD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Devin has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 9 entries — a stretch where Devin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Devin is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Devin would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Devin has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Devin has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Devin's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Devin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Devin is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Devin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Devin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Devin has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Biren Fong: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Biren Fong has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Selina Lei has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Devin's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Devin has shown up 5 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Devin is not just losing locally — Devin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shao family travels cross-country so Devin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devin has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Devin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Devin entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Devin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Devin story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Devin comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Devin, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

dhev_sudheer.name: Dhev Sudheer
dhev_sudheer.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Dhev Sudheer is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the final four 4 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Dhev is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Dhev has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Isha Haldankar (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Harshith Yoganand (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Dhev has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Dhev has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Dhev brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
dhev_sudheer.roast:
Allow us to introduce Dhev Sudheer, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 29 events across 11 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 29-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Dhev's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Dhev keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Dhev saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Dhev would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Dhev has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Dhev has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Dhev's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dhev plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Dhev has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dhev's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Varad V Vaishnav has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dhev is not just losing locally — Dhev is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sudheer family travels cross-country so Dhev can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhev has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhev showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Dhev traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Dhev entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Dhev paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 29 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Dhev is not most humans. Dhev is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Dhev the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Dhev. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Dhruv has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dhruv's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Kelly Hu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Dhruv entered 3 events with results ranging from top-33 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Dhruv's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Dhruv and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Dhruv has shown up 4 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dhruv is not just losing locally — Dhruv is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Pediredla family travels cross-country so Dhruv can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhruv has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhruv showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Dhruv traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Dhruv entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Dhruv paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 33 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Dhruv is not most humans. Dhruv is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Dhruv the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Dhruv. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

Because behind those headlines is a 57-entry career across 22 tournaments with a 7% win rate, which means 53 times — 53! — Dhvani went home without a trophy. And to be fair, Dhvani has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Dhvani promised and what Dhvani has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Dhvani actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Dhvani is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In Girls' Doubles, Dhvani has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Dhvani chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically. Can we talk about Dhvani and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 1 losses. Dhvani can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Dhvani still cannot reach it.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Dhvani's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Dhvani has reached 5 championship matches across this career. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Dhvani entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Dhvani paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Dhvani's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 4 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 53 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Dhvani is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Girls' Doubles. Will Dhvani figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Dhvani's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

A true triple-threat, Dhyuti competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

dhyuti_aruru.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Dhyuti Aruru, from Texas, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Dhyuti's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Dhyuti keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Dhyuti saga began at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dhyuti plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Dhyuti has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Advik Rai has emotionally recovered.

Dhyuti has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhyuti has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhyuti showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15, which means Dhyuti traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Dhyuti entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Dhyuti paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Dhyuti keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Dhyuti has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Dhyuti, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

diksha_chandrasekaran.name: Diksha Chandrasekaran
diksha_chandrasekaran.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Diksha Chandrasekaran has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Diksha's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Diksha has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Diksha competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Diksha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Mark w Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5)
  • Sophia Sun (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U15 (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Diksha has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Diksha has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Diksha is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
diksha_chandrasekaran.roast:
Allow us to introduce Diksha Chandrasekaran, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 80 career entries in 28 tournaments, Diksha has 5 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 15 entries where Diksha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 80 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Diksha saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Diksha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Diksha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Diksha is 0-for-16. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Remember when Diksha used to win GD U15? Because Diksha sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Diksha took gold at 2024 DFW South OLC, and since then? Best finish: 2nd. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

Can we talk about Diksha and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 1 losses. Diksha can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Diksha still cannot reach it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diksha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Diksha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Diksha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Diksha's texts. The go-to partner is Yifei Zhang (21 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Jessica Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Diksha's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Diksha entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Diksha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Diksha and said 'nah.'

Diksha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Diksha is not just losing locally — Diksha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chandrasekaran family travels cross-country so Diksha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diksha has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diksha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Diksha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Diksha entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Diksha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles, 80 entries, and 75 that ended without a trophy. A player who proof that glory has an expiration date and Diksha's is past due. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Diksha looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Diksha. We will be watching.

Out of Massachusetts, Disha Vijayakumar has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 36 entries across 14 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Disha competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Disha adapts to anyone:
  • Sourish Yanamandra (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

Disha has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Disha isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
