disha_vijayakumar.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Disha Vijayakumar, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Disha has 36 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Disha saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE NJBC CRC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Disha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Disha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Disha is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Disha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Disha is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Disha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Disha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Disha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Disha's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Sourish Yanamandra: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sourish Yanamandra has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Dwijesh Makireddi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Disha's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Disha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Disha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Disha has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Disha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15, which means Disha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Disha entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Disha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Disha story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Disha comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Disha, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

divaakar_prabu_mahesh.name: Divaakar Prabu Mahesh
divaakar_prabu_mahesh.summary:
Representing North Carolina, Divaakar Prabu Mahesh has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 6 entries in 4 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Divaakar competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Divaakar has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
divaakar_prabu_mahesh.roast:
Step right up and meet Divaakar Prabu Mahesh, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Divaakar is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South OLC (TBTT), and so far, the only thing Divaakar has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Divaakar saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Divaakar has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Divaakar has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Divaakar showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BS U17, which means Divaakar traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Season after season, Divaakar keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Divaakar has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Divaakar, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

divith_pradeep_handral.name: Divith Pradeep Handral
divith_pradeep_handral.summary: Divith Pradeep Handral is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Divith's.
divith_pradeep_handral.roast:
Step right up and meet Divith Pradeep Handral, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Divith is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, and so far, the only thing Divith has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Divith saga began at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Divith is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Divith's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Divith is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Divith has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Divith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in BS U11, which means Divith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Divith is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Divith wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

diya_abbineni.name: Diya Abbineni
diya_abbineni.summary:
Representing Texas, Diya Abbineni is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U13 level, with 9 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Diya especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Diya has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Tanvika Kantipudi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5)
  • Rayan Shyam (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Diya has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
diya_abbineni.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Diya Abbineni, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Diya is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, and so far, the only thing Diya has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Diya saga began at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Diya has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diya has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U13, which means Diya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Diya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Diya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

With only 9 entries on the books, Diya is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Diya. We will have more material. We always do.

diya_rajan.name: Diya Rajan
diya_rajan.summary:
Representing Illinois, Diya Rajan is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U19 level, with 17 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Diya especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Diya has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Aditya Prasad Iyer (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, spanning 3 seasons)

Diya has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Diya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
diya_rajan.roast:
Step right up and meet Diya Rajan, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Diya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Diya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Diya saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Aditya Prasad Iyer: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aditya Prasad Iyer has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Aditya Prasad Iyer has emotionally recovered.

Diya has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diya has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Diya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Diya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Diya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Diya keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Diya has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Diya knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

diya_shetty.name: Diya Shetty
diya_shetty.summary:
Representing Virginia, Diya Shetty is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Diya in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Diya is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Diya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 4 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior Championships competitor (2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U15 GD) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GS U15, GD U15) • 10 ORC titles.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Diya adapts to anyone:
  • Sanjanaa Shyam (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Aadya Pitani (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (3 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

Diya has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Diya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Diya has been seeded #1 or #2 in 11 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 4 seasons and 11 titles, Diya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
diya_shetty.roast:
Step right up and meet Diya Shetty, from Virginia, currently competing at the U17 level. Hold on — Diya has competed at the Pan American Junior Championships. That is an international tournament where you literally represent your country. Sounds amazing, right? Except Diya finished with a best of top-9, which means Diya flew to another country, wore the red-white-and-blue, stepped on court as a representative of the United States... and got bounced before the podium was even set up. Patriotism is beautiful. The results were not. Moving on to the other 60 career entries.

The Diya saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13), where Diya burst onto the scene with a title in GD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Diya has delivered on some of those promises: 11 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Diya promised and what Diya has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 6 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Diya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Diya is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Here is a fun detail: in GS U13, Diya used to be seeded #1 (at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13)). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC). Tournament committees are literally telling Diya 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Diya reaches a final. Diya has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Diya entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Diya does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Diya has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 11 wins means Diya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 5 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Diya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Diya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Diya's texts. The go-to partner is Vincent Chen (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Darsh Jain has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Diya entered 8 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Diya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Diya and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Diya 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Diya has been seeded in 16 events and underperformed the seeding 7 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Diya takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, and finishing 65th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Diya. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Diya of is playing it safe. Diya has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Diya has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Diya has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Diya is not just losing locally — Diya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 18% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shetty family travels cross-country so Diya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Diya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Diya entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 65th. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Diya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Diya's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 11 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 50 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Diya is a player who has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning. Will Diya figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Diya's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

diyaa_sarath.name: Diyaa Sarath
diyaa_sarath.summary:
A Georgia competitor, Diyaa Sarath has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Diyaa's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Diyaa has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Diyaa is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Diyaa has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Raymond Haiwen Song (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Namratha Nishal (current partner) — currently teaming up in U15 (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Diyaa has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Diyaa has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Diyaa has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
diyaa_sarath.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Diyaa Sarath file just landed on our desk. Diyaa hails from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 40 career entries in 15 tournaments, Diyaa has 2 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 19 entries where Diyaa drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 40 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Diyaa saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Diyaa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 17 entries — a stretch where Diyaa actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Diyaa is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Diyaa would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Diyaa has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Diyaa has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Diyaa's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diyaa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Diyaa can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Diyaa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Diyaa's texts. The go-to partner is Eshan Neog (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Sai Sahasra Gajula has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Diyaa's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Diyaa entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Diyaa's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Diyaa and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Diyaa has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Diyaa has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Diyaa is not just losing locally — Diyaa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sarath family travels cross-country so Diyaa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diyaa has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diyaa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Diyaa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Diyaa entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Diyaa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Diyaa's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 38 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Diyaa is a player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. Will Diyaa figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Diyaa's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

dwijesh_makireddi.name: Dwijesh Makireddi
dwijesh_makireddi.summary:
Out of Massachusetts, Dwijesh Makireddi has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Dwijesh's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Dwijesh has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Dwijesh especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Dwijesh adapts to anyone:
  • Varin Karri (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Nidhi Sujin (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD)
  • Venkata Shashank Kondaveeti (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Jessica Zhang (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

Dwijesh has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Dwijesh has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Dwijesh consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 6 titles, Dwijesh has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
dwijesh_makireddi.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Dwijesh Makireddi, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Dwijesh has 63 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 10% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Dwijesh saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Dwijesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Dwijesh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

BS U17 used to be Dwijesh's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 NE MassBad OLC era. Now Dwijesh is grinding for a top-3 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dwijesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Dwijesh can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Dwijesh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dwijesh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Adithya S Burisetty: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Adithya S Burisetty has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Disha Vijayakumar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Dwijesh's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Dwijesh has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Dwijesh a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Dwijesh has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 NE NJBC CRC in BS U17, where Dwijesh was seeded #1 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Dwijesh has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dwijesh is not just losing locally — Dwijesh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Makireddi family travels cross-country so Dwijesh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dwijesh has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dwijesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Dwijesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Dwijesh entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Dwijesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Dwijesh's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 6 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 57 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Dwijesh is a player who proof that glory has an expiration date and Dwijesh's is past due. Will Dwijesh figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Dwijesh's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

dylan_ji_daniels.roast:
Allow us to introduce Dylan Ji Daniels, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Dylan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Dylan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Dylan saga began at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dylan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Dylan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Tsz Wai Lam has emotionally recovered.

Dylan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dylan has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dylan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Dylan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Dylan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Dylan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

4 seasons. 14 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Dylan's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)
